name: lentil mushroom stew
instructions: Sauté the soffrito (yellow onion, celery, carrots) in olive oil. Add pureed portabella mushrooms and cook them down. Add exactly ONE finely chopped tomato — this dish should NOT taste like tomatoes; the tomato is structural body, not a flavor headliner. Cook the tomato down. Deglaze with white wine and let it reduce. Add lentils, FINELY CHOPPED POTATOES (added at the same time as the lentils — see notes; non-optional), rosemary AND/OR bay leaf (need one or the other — see notes), black pepper, and enough vegetable stock to cover. Slow cook covered for a long time. If the broth is too thin at the end, stir in a slurry of starch to thicken.
notes:
ONE TOMATO, NOT MANY (TOMATO AS A BUDDY): this dish should not taste like tomatoes. A single finely chopped tomato is enough — it's there for tomato BALANCE with the mushroom rather than for tomato flavor. The mushroom is the headliner and it wants a buddy, but not a loud one. Pureeing the tomato or adding more pulls the dish toward a tomato-lentil soup, which is a different recipe entirely.

NO TOMATO PASTE: tomato paste in particular pushes the dish too tomato-flavored. It's concentrated, sweet, and amplifies tomato presence beyond what this recipe wants. Stick to the single fresh chopped tomato.

NO THYME: thyme is inappropriate here. The dish is mushroom-and-lentil dominant — a relatively monolithic savory profile — so it wants rosemary (which adds a second dimension) rather than thyme (which unifies scattered flavors). See the herbs and greens article on the thyme-vs-rosemary axis.

NO OLD BAY: removed from the recipe. Old bay belongs in italian-minestrone-soup-shaped dishes that explicitly choose it as one of the three flavoring directions. This dish has its own flavor backbone (mushroom + lentil + wine + rosemary) and doesn't want the old bay axis on top.

POTATOES ARE NON-OPTIONAL — FINELY CHOPPED, ADDED WITH THE LENTILS: potatoes are load-bearing in this stew, not an optional add-on. Chop them FINELY (small dice — not large chunks) so they cook at roughly the same rate as the lentils, and add them at the same time as the lentils. The potatoes contribute two things the dish needs: (1) starchy body that thickens the broth structurally as it simmers, replacing much of the need for the starch slurry finish, and (2) a starchy carb texture in each bite that softens the otherwise-monolithic lentil-and-mushroom mouthfeel. Without potatoes the stew reads thin and one-textured. Finely chopping — not chunk-cutting — matters because you want the potato distributed through every spoonful rather than showing up as discrete chunks.

ROSEMARY OR BAY LEAF — YOU NEED ONE, EITHER WORKS: previously rosemary was the canonical herb here. Now bay leaf is a real alternative — either works, and they play the same second-dimension role against the mushroom-and-lentil base. If you use rosemary, bay leaf becomes optional (rosemary alone can carry the dish). If you use bay leaf, rosemary becomes optional. You need at least ONE of them; skipping both leaves the dish flat, which is the same failure mode the original recipe warned about.
- Rosemary: piney and assertive; adds a distinct second dimension that reads european-forest-forward. Use lightly at full quantity dominates.
- Bay leaf: rounder and more background; adds warm depth without pushing a specific direction. Toss in whole; fish out before serving.
- Both together also fine but the effect is diminishing returns — one usually does the job.

HERB DOSING:
- Rosemary helps a little. Use lightly — it's the second-dimension herb that makes the dish less flat, but rosemary at full quantity would dominate.
- Thyme is inappropriate.
- Don't reach for other herbs by default.

WHITE WINE IS LOAD-BEARING: a real white wine deglaze helps a lot. The acid and depth it adds are what lifts the dish above a generic lentil stew. Don't substitute vinegar.

STARCH FINISH (OPTIONAL): if the broth ends up thinner than you want, stir in a small starch slurry at the end to thicken. Not always needed — the pureed mushrooms and lentils usually carry enough body — but it's the right move when the soup is too brothy.

STOCK IS PRIMARY HERE: this dish wants a real vegetable stock as the liquid, not bouillon cubes. The mushroom-and-lentil profile benefits from stock's gentler, more layered body — bouillon cubes are too concentrated and salty and push the soup toward a generic vegetable-broth lentil flavor. Use a quality vegetable stock as the cooking liquid; if all you have is a bouillon cube, dilute it heavily.
